text: The surgeon performs a single incision distal gastrectomy with delta anastomosis, starting with a 2.5 to 4 cm skin incision for the single incision port. A glove port is inserted, and a needle passer with a suture is introduced below the sternum. The suture is left in place, penetrates the falciform ligament, and is retrieved outside. The lesser sac is opened, and the suture is fixed to the gastrophrenic ligament.

A gauze is placed between the suture and the liver to prevent hepatic injury. The surgeon performs a partial omentectomy, opening the lesser sac to visualize the posterior wall of the stomach. The greater curvature is cleared above the predicted resection site, and the left gastroepiploic vessels are ligated using a bipolar energy device. Dissection of the right side omentum is carefully carried out, avoiding injury to the colon.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and dissects the number six lymph node area around the right gastroepiploic vessels. The head of the pancreas is visualized, and the right gastroepiploic vessels located above the pancreas are carefully dissected and ligated.

The surgeon clears the greater curvature of the stomach, inserting gauze beneath the dissected pylorus for safe dissection. The lesser curvature is also cleared, and the right gastric artery is identified and ligated. The pylorus is rotated 90 degrees to ensure a transverse resection line, and the distal end of the stomach is transected with a linear stapler. Suprapancreatic lymph node dissection begins around the common hepatic artery, continuing along the pancreas's upper border and around the celiac artery. Lymph node enlargement is observed, and dissection above the common hepatic artery is completed. The coronary vein and left gastric artery are dissected and ligated.
The surgeon ligates the anterior vagus nerve and proceeds to dissect the number three lymph node area.

The surgeon transects the stomach at the marked site and pulls the proximal stomach towards the duodenal stump to ensure no tension. A hole is created on the greater curvature of the proximal stomach and another on the medial side of the duodenal stump. Sutures are placed on both the duodenal stump and proximal stomach for traction. The 45mm cartridge side of a linear stapler is inserted into the hole on the proximal stomach, and the stapler is pulled towards the duodenal stump. The anvil side of the stapler is inserted into the hole on the duodenal stump, and both structures are everted to align their posterior surfaces. The stapler is fired, and tagging sutures are placed on the superior and posterior anastomosis lines. A linear stapler with a 60mm cartridge is used to close the entry hole, completing the delta anastomosis.
